summary: Julia Evans pushes back against gatekeeping advice she received about learning kernel programming and offers four accessible paths for getting started. She was told that without a professional need, there's no reason to learn about the kernel, and that subscribing to LKML was the way in. Instead, she proposes writing your own OS (starting from existing projects), writing kernel modules, doing a Linux kernel internship through outreach programs, and reading kernel source code with cross-referencing tools. Each path is presented as genuinely fun and achievable without deep expertise.
key_points: Traditional advice about kernel learning is gatekeeping and unhelpful — telling people to subscribe to LKML or only work on mainline code discourages beginners
Writing your own OS is less scary than it sounds when you start from something that already works and make small incremental changes
Kernel modules can be as simple as 18 lines of code, making them an accessible entry point to kernel development
Reading kernel source code is surprisingly approachable — you won't understand everything, but tools like LXR make it fun to explore familiar system calls like chmod
Outreach programs like OPW provide structured paths into kernel development with mentorship and real patch acceptance
money_quotes: If you don't *need* to understand the kernel for your work, why would you try?
This was really, really, really not helpful to me.
Remember that your operating system doesn't have to be big and professional – if you make it turn the screen purple instead of red and then maybe make it print it a limerick, you've already won.
I don't really understand what the `__init` AND `__exit` macros do, but I can use them!
I felt kind of dumb for not understanding things, but then every single person I talked to was like 'yeah, it's the Linux kernel, of course!'
Most of the code I read I find pretty opaque, but some of it (like this chmod code) is a little bit understandable.
key_insight: The Linux kernel isn't scary if you ignore the gatekeepers and find your own fun, incremental path in — whether that's turning a screen purple, writing an 18-line module, or reading chmod's source code.
